title: Small Wart-Like Growth on Dog's Prepuce: A Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discovered a small bump on your dog's prepuce? Understand common causes like papillomas, urgency levels, and why photos help your vet.
content_html:
Potential Causes of Preputial Growths

Finding a small, wart-like bump on your dog's prepuce (the skin covering the penis) can be unsettling. In many cases, these growths are benign canine viral papillomas, often referred to as dog warts. Other possibilities include skin tags, sebaceous cysts, or occasionally more serious localized skin tumors. Because this area is sensitive and prone to friction, even benign growths can sometimes become irritated.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a small, isolated growth in this area is generally Low. If your dog is acting normally, eating well, and urinating without difficulty, there is no immediate need for an emergency visit. You should, however, schedule a routine examination with your veterinarian to have the mass identified. If the growth begins to bleed excessively, changes color rapidly, or appears to obstruct the urethral opening, you should move the appointment up.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of the growth is one of the most helpful things you can do. A photo provides a baseline for your veterinarian to monitor if the growth is changing in size, shape, or texture. It also allows a triage professional to provide better immediate guidance and helps the clinical team prepare for your dog's specific needs before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Should I try to remove or pop the growth at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze, cut, or remove a growth yourself. This can cause significant pain, severe bleeding, and secondary infections, especially in such a sensitive area.

faqs.question: What signs indicate the growth is becoming a problem?
faqs.answer: Watch for signs of discomfort such as excessive licking, redness, discharge, or if your dog seems to be straining while urinating. Rapid increase in size is also a reason for a prompt veterinary visit.

faqs.question: Are these growths contagious to other dogs?
faqs.answer: If the growth is a viral papilloma, it can be contagious to other dogs through direct contact. It is best to keep your dog away from communal areas like dog parks until a veterinarian confirms the nature of the growth.
